annotations.label: Discrete mathematics

anchor.snippet: Discrete mathematics is the study of mathematical structures

annotations.note: A name for a field of mathematics, not a formal mathematical statement that Mathlib could state.

annotations.label: Finite mathematics

anchor.snippet: The term finite mathematics is sometimes applied to parts of the field of discrete mathematics

annotations.note: An informal label for a curricular area, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Theoretical computer science

anchor.snippet: Theoretical computer science includes areas of discrete mathematics relevant to computing

annotations.note: A description of an academic field rather than a mathematical proposition.

annotations.label: Information theory

anchor.snippet: Information theory involves the quantification of information

annotations.note: The field as such is not a statement, but related infrastructure quantifying information (Kullback–Leibler divergence) is formalized.

annotations.label: Coding theory

anchor.snippet: Closely related is coding theory which is used to design efficient and reliable data transmission

annotations.note: Coding-theory infrastructure such as Hamming distance (and Kraft–McMillan) exists, though the field itself is not a single statement.

annotations.label: Logic

anchor.snippet: Logic is the study of the principles of valid reasoning and inference

annotations.note: A description of a discipline, not a formal mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Peirce's law is a theorem

anchor.snippet: Peirce's law

annotations.note: Mathlib's `peirce` proves exactly ((a → b) → a) → a.

annotations.label: Set theory

anchor.snippet: Set theory is the branch of mathematics that studies sets

annotations.note: The branch is not a statement, but its central object `Set` is formalized.

annotations.label: Combinatorics

anchor.snippet: Combinatorics studies the ways in which discrete structures can be combined or arranged

annotations.note: A field name covering many topics, not a single formalizable proposition.

annotations.label: Enumerative combinatorics

anchor.snippet: Enumerative combinatorics concentrates on counting the number of certain combinatorial objects

annotations.note: A subfield description rather than a stateable theorem or definition.

annotations.label: Analytic combinatorics

anchor.snippet: Analytic combinatorics concerns the enumeration

annotations.note: A methodological subfield, not a formal statement.

annotations.label: Topological combinatorics

anchor.snippet: Topological combinatorics concerns the use of techniques from topology

annotations.note: A subfield description, not a formalizable proposition.

annotations.label: Design theory

anchor.snippet: Design theory is a study of combinatorial designs

annotations.note: No combinatorial-design (block design / Steiner system) theory was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Partition theory

anchor.snippet: Partition theory studies various enumeration and asymptotic problems related to integer partitions

annotations.note: Integer partitions are formalized as `Nat.Partition`, though the subfield itself is not a statement.

annotations.label: Order theory

anchor.snippet: Order theory is the study of partially ordered sets

annotations.note: The central object, the partially ordered set `PartialOrder`, is formalized.

annotations.label: Graph theory

anchor.snippet: Graph theory, the study of graphs and networks

annotations.note: Graphs are formalized via `SimpleGraph`, though the field as a whole is not a statement.

annotations.label: Number theory

anchor.snippet: Number theory is concerned with the properties of numbers in general

annotations.note: A broad field name rather than a single formalizable proposition.

annotations.label: Discrete algebraic structures

anchor.snippet: Algebraic structures occur as both discrete examples and continuous examples

annotations.note: A general remark about algebraic structures, not a specific formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Sequence in discrete calculus

anchor.section: Calculus of finite differences, discrete analysis, and discrete calculus
anchor.snippet: a function defined on an interval of the integers is usually called a sequence

annotations.note: Sequences are just functions `ℕ → α` in Mathlib with no dedicated 'sequence' definition matching this informal description.

annotations.label: Difference equations

anchor.section: Calculus of finite differences, discrete analysis, and discrete calculus
anchor.snippet: Difference equations are similar to differential equations

annotations.note: No general theory of difference equations was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Time scale calculus

anchor.section: Calculus of finite differences, discrete analysis, and discrete calculus
anchor.snippet: The time scale calculus is a unification of the theory of difference equations

annotations.note: Time scale calculus is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Discrete geometry

anchor.snippet: Discrete geometry and combinatorial geometry are about combinatorial properties of discrete collections of geometrical objects

annotations.note: A field description, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Point as spectrum of local ring

anchor.snippet: a point together with a neighborhood around it

annotations.note: The prime spectrum `PrimeSpectrum` is formalized, but the informal identification of a point with the spectrum of a local ring is not stated as such.

annotations.label: Discrete modelling

anchor.snippet: In discrete modelling, discrete formulae are fit to data

annotations.note: An applied modelling notion, not a formalizable mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Four color theorem

anchor.snippet: attempts to prove the four color theorem

annotations.note: No statement or proof of the four color theorem exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gödel's second incompleteness theorem

anchor.snippet: Gödel's second incompleteness theorem

annotations.note: Only Gödel's β-function (`Mathlib/Logic/Godel/GodelBetaFunction.lean`) is present; the incompleteness theorems are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hilbert's tenth problem (Matiyasevich)

anchor.snippet: Hilbert's tenth problem was to determine whether a given polynomial Diophantine equation

annotations.note: Mathlib has `pow_dioph` (the power function is Diophantine, a key step in Matiyasevich's theorem) but not the full DPRM resolution / undecidability of Hilbert's tenth problem.

annotations.label: P = NP problem

anchor.snippet: the P = NP problem

annotations.note: No formalization of complexity classes P/NP or the P = NP question exists in Mathlib.
